title: Canard aux cerises
subtitle: Duck legs braised with cherries and red wine
excerpt: Duck legs browned and then braised in red wine and stock with sour cherries and a splash of kirsch. The cherry sauce reduces around the duck until it is glossy and slightly sweet, cutting through the richness of the fat. A French bistro classic, best in early July when fresh Morello cherries are available.
sourceNotes: Canard aux cerises is documented in classical French cuisine as a fruit-braised duck dish, exploiting the affinity between duck fat and sharp, acidic fruit. The dish appears in nineteenth-century French cookbooks and is related to the traditional use of sour cherries (Morello) with game birds. Elizabeth David includes a version in 'French Provincial Cooking' (1960).

recipe.makeAheadNotes: The braise can be made a day ahead and reheated gently; skim any solidified fat from the surface before reheating.

content.text: Duck fat and sour fruit have a long relationship in French cooking. The fat needs something acidic to cut through it; fresh cherries (and Morello cherries in particular) provide exactly that. The trick is balance: enough cherries to season the sauce but not so many that the dish becomes a compote. Add the cherries in two stages: half at the start to dissolve into the braising liquid, half fifteen minutes before the end to keep their shape and colour.

Duck legs are the right cut for this braise — they have enough fat and collagen to hold through ninety minutes of cooking without drying. Breast would be done in twenty minutes and dry in thirty. Kirsch (cherry eau-de-vie) adds a finishing note if you have it; brandy works as a substitute.

items.prepNote: fresh Morello or sour cherries, pitted; or use jarred Morello cherries in syrup

content.text: Season the {{duck-legs}} generously. Place them skin-side down in a cold casserole. Turn the heat to medium and render the fat for 10 minutes until the skin is deep golden. Turn and brown the other side for 4 minutes. Remove the legs and pour off most of the rendered fat, leaving about 1 tablespoon.

Add the {{shallot}} and {{garlic}} to the casserole over medium heat and cook for 3 minutes. Add half the {{cherries}} and cook for 2 minutes, pressing them lightly so they begin to break down. Pour in the {{kirsch}} if using and let it bubble for 30 seconds. Pour in the {{red-wine}} of red wine and {{stock-chicken}} of chicken stock. Add the {{thyme}} of thyme. Return the duck legs skin-side up, cover, and braise over low heat for 1 hour.
Add the remaining cherries and finish

Remove the duck legs. Discard the thyme. Bring the braising liquid to a boil and reduce for 5 minutes until slightly thickened. Add the remaining {{cherries}} and simmer for 3 minutes. Take the pan off the heat and whisk in the cold {{unsalted-butter}} of butter to gloss the sauce. Return the duck to the pan, spoon the sauce over, and serve.

items.cause: Cherries are sweet rather than sour, or jarred cherries in heavy syrup used without draining
items.fix: Add a splash of red wine vinegar or lemon juice; Morello cherries are the ideal — sweet cherries lack the tartness that balances the duck fat

items.symptom: Cherry skins have disintegrated and the sauce is grainy
items.cause: All cherries added at the start and cooked for the full hour
items.fix: Always add cherries in two stages: half to dissolve into the sauce, half at the end to hold their shape

items.symptom: Duck fat makes the sauce greasy
items.cause: Too much fat not poured off before braising
items.fix: Pour off all but a tablespoon of fat after browning; skim any fat from the surface of the braising liquid before reducing

items.symptom: Skin has lost its crispness by serving
items.fix: Keep the legs skin-side up throughout; the skin steams but stays above the liquid level

content.text: Canard aux cerises is part of the French tradition of braising duck with fruit (cherries, oranges, olives, prunes) that uses acidity and sweetness to cut the richness of the rendered fat. The cherry version is associated with early summer, when Morello cherries are briefly in season in France and the UK. In the French kitchen, the duck-and-cherry combination appears in both the bistro register and the haute cuisine register: the same flavour logic expressed at different levels of elaboration. The home version, as here, is the bistro register: brown the duck, braise it in wine, add cherries, reduce, serve.
